[{"role": "system", "content": "# TASK
1. Analyze the provided labeled examples to identify the observable visual features that distinguish each class.
2. Examine the Query Image and compare it strictly against the provided examples.
3. Determine which label from the provided options best matches the Query Image.

# INSTRUCTIONS
- Base your decision only on observable visual evidence in the examples and the Query Image.
- Use the examples to infer discriminative visual patterns for each class.
- Do not use external world knowledge, hidden assumptions, or speculative attributes.
- Use only labels from the provided final options list.
- Choose exactly one final label.
- Output only the requested XML tags and not any aditional ones.
- Do not output any text outside the XML tags.
- Follow the condition-specific XML blocks first, then output exactly one final <response> block.
- The final line of the entire answer must be <response>final_class</response>.
- Never omit the final <response> block, even when another explanation block is required.

First list the critical observable features present only in the Query Image.
Then formulate a knowledge base (kb) with a small set of IF-THEN rules grounded in observable patterns from the labeled examples in the support images and the Query Image, for all classes. Try to unify the conditions so that there is only one rule per class.
Rules must refer only to visible features.
Then check which rule(s) are best satisfied by the Query Image.

<features>
- observable feature: feature value
- observable feature: feature value
- observable feature: feature value
</features>
<kb>
- IF [observable visual features = feature value AND/OR observable visual features = feature value] THEN [class1]
- IF [observable visual features = feature value] THEN [class2]
</kb>

<rule_check>
- State which rule(s) from the kb fires for the Query Image and briefly explain why,
referring only to the features listed above. Do not discuss other rules or introduce new features.
</rule_check>

<response>final_class</response>

raw_response_text:
<features>
- animal type: dog
- fur color: brown, black, and white
- fur length: long
- face mask: black
- tongue: visible and pink
</features>
<kb>
- IF [animal type = dog AND fur color = brown, black, and white AND fur length = long AND face mask = black] THEN [19]
- IF [animal type = cat AND fur color = cream and dark brown/black AND face mask = dark brown/black] THEN [32]
- IF [animal type = cat AND fur color = white and light brown/grey AND fur length = long AND mouth = open with tongue visible] THEN [26]
- IF [animal type = cat AND fur color = grey tabby and white/cream AND fur length = long] THEN [20]
</kb>
<rule_check>
- The rule for class 19 fires because the Query Image shows a dog with long brown, black, and white fur, and a distinct black face mask.
</rule_check>
<response>19</response>

# TASK
1. Analyze the provided labeled examples to identify the observable visual features that distinguish each class.
2. Examine the Query Image and compare it strictly against the provided examples.
3. Determine which label from the provided options best matches the Query Image.

# INSTRUCTIONS
- Base your decision only on observable visual evidence in the examples and the Query Image.
- Use the examples to infer discriminative visual patterns for each class.
- Do not use external world knowledge, hidden assumptions, or speculative attributes.
- Use only labels from the provided final options list.
- Choose exactly one final label.
- Output only the requested XML tags and not any aditional ones.
- Do not output any text outside the XML tags.
- Follow the condition-specific XML blocks first, then output exactly one final <response> block.
- The final line of the entire answer must be <response>final_class</response>.
- Never omit the final <response> block, even when another explanation block is required.

First list the critical observable features present only in the Query Image.
Then formulate a knowledge base (kb) with a small set of IF-THEN rules grounded in observable patterns from the labeled examples in the support images and the Query Image, for all classes. Try to unify the conditions so that there is only one rule per class.
Rules must refer only to visible features.
Then check which rule(s) are best satisfied by the Query Image.

<features>
- observable feature: feature value
- observable feature: feature value
- observable feature: feature value
</features>
<kb>
- IF [observable visual features = feature value AND/OR observable visual features = feature value] THEN [class1]
- IF [observable visual features = feature value] THEN [class2]
</kb>

<rule_check>
- State which rule(s) from the kb fires for the Query Image and briefly explain why,
referring only to the features listed above. Do not discuss other rules or introduce new features.
</rule_check>

<response>final_class</response>

The content of <response> must be exactly one label copied verbatim from the provided options list.

raw_response_text:
<features>
- fur color pattern: light body with dark extremities (face, ears, paws, tail)
- eye color: blue
- animal type: cat
</features>
<kb>
- IF [animal type = cat AND fur color pattern = light body with dark extremities AND eye color = blue] THEN [32]
- IF [animal type = cat AND fur color pattern = mostly white/cream with some light brown/grey patches AND long fur] THEN [26]
- IF [animal type = cat AND fur color pattern = tabby (grey/black stripes) with long fur] THEN [20]
- IF [animal type = dog AND fur color pattern = reddish-brown with long fur] THEN [19]
</kb>
<rule_check>
- The rule for class 32 fires because the Query Image shows a cat with a light body and dark extremities (face, ears, paws, tail), and blue eyes, which matches the observable features of the examples for class 32.
</rule_check>
<response>32</response>

raw_response_text:
<features>
- fur color: predominantly white with some light brown/grey markings on face and tail
- eye color: blue
- fur length: long and fluffy
- body shape: somewhat stocky, sitting upright with front paws tucked in
- tail: long and fluffy, with a darker tip
</features>
<kb>
- IF [fur color is predominantly white/cream with some light brown/grey markings AND eye color is blue AND fur is long and fluffy] THEN [26]
- IF [fur color is predominantly light brown/tan with dark brown markings on face, ears, paws, and tail AND eye color is blue] THEN [32]
- IF [fur color is a mix of grey, black, and white tabby patterns AND fur is long and fluffy AND ears have prominent tufts] THEN [20]
- IF [fur color is reddish-brown/tan AND fur is long and dense AND body is large and muscular] THEN [19]
</kb>
<rule_check>
- The rule for class 26 fires because the Query Image shows a cat with predominantly white/cream fur, some light brown/grey markings on its face and tail, and blue eyes, consistent with the features observed in the example for class 26.
</rule_check>
<response>26</response>

raw_response_text:
<features>
- animal type: cat
- fur pattern: tabby stripes
- fur color: brown, black, white
- fur length: long
- ear tufts: present
- tail: long and bushy
</features>
<kb>
- IF [animal type = dog AND fur length = long AND fur color = brown/tan] THEN [19]
- IF [animal type = cat AND fur pattern = colorpoint AND fur length = short to medium] THEN [32]
- IF [animal type = cat AND fur length = very long AND fur color = light (white/cream) with some darker patches AND facial expression = mouth open] THEN [26]
- IF [animal type = cat AND fur length = long AND fur pattern = tabby stripes AND ear tufts = present] THEN [20]
</kb>
<rule_check>
- The rule for class 20 fires because the Query Image shows a cat with long fur, tabby stripes, and visible ear tufts.
</rule_check>
<response>20</response>
